Pepper___Bacterial_Spot.name: Bacterial Spot
Pepper___Bacterial_Spot.description: Bacterial spot of pepper is caused by Xanthomonas campestris. It is one of the most devastating pepper diseases in warm, wet climates and affects leaves, stems, and fruits.
symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves that turn brown with yellow halos
Lesions may merge causing large irregular dead areas
Dark, raised, scabby lesions on fruit surface reducing marketability
Severe defoliation in warm, rainy weather
treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides preventively during wet weather
Use certified disease-free transplants and seed
Avoid overhead irrigation to reduce leaf wetness duration
Remove and destroy infected plant debris
Rotate crops for 2-3 years away from pepper and tomato

Pepper___Healthy.name: Healthy Pepper
Pepper___Healthy.description: No disease detected. The pepper plant appears healthy with no visible symptoms of infection.
symptoms: No visible symptoms.
treatment: Continue standard agronomic practices
Monitor regularly for early signs of disease or pest pressure
Maintain optimal soil moisture, nutrition, and staking

Potato___Early_Blight.name: Early Blight
Potato___Early_Blight.description: Early blight is caused by the fungus Alternaria solani. It is a common and widespread potato disease that affects foliage and tubers, especially in warm humid conditions.
symptoms: Dark brown circular lesions with concentric rings forming a target-board pattern
Lesions surrounded by yellow chlorotic areas
Lower, older leaves affected first; disease progresses upward
Severe defoliation reduces tuber size and overall yield
treatment: Apply fungicides (chlorothalonil, mancozeb, azoxystrobin) preventively
Remove and destroy infected plant debris after harvest
Use certified disease-free seed potatoes
Maintain adequate plant nutrition to reduce stress-related susceptibility

Potato___Healthy.name: Healthy Potato
Potato___Healthy.description: No disease detected. The potato plant appears healthy with no visible symptoms of infection.
symptoms: No visible symptoms.
treatment: Continue standard agronomic practices
Monitor regularly for early signs of disease or pest pressure
Maintain optimal soil moisture and nutrition

Potato___Late_Blight.name: Late Blight
Potato___Late_Blight.description: Late blight is caused by the oomycete Phytophthora infestans and is the most destructive potato disease globally. It caused the Irish Famine in the 1840s and spreads extremely rapidly in cool, wet weather.
symptoms: Water-soaked, pale green lesions on leaves that rapidly turn brown to black
White fuzzy sporulation on the underside of leaves in humid conditions
Dark brown, greasy patches on stems that can girdle and kill the plant
Tuber rot: reddish-brown granular internal tissue rot
treatment: Apply protectant fungicides (mancozeb, chlorothalonil) before infection
Use systemic fungicides (metalaxyl, cymoxanil) after early detection
Plant certified late blight-resistant potato varieties
Destroy all infected plant material and volunteer plants
Avoid overhead irrigation — use drip irrigation if possible

Tomato___Bacterial_Spot.name: Bacterial Spot
Tomato___Bacterial_Spot.description: Bacterial spot is caused by Xanthomonas species. It affects leaves, stems, and fruits and is favored by warm, wet conditions. It is one of the most common and damaging tomato diseases worldwide.
symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves that enlarge and turn dark brown to black
Lesions have yellow halos and irregular margins
Dark, raised, scabby lesions on fruit surface
Severe defoliation and fruit blemishes reduce marketability significantly
treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides preventively during wet weather
Use disease-free transplants and certified seed
Avoid overhead irrigation to reduce leaf wetness duration
Remove and destroy infected plant material
Rotate crops — do not plant tomatoes in same location for 2-3 years

Tomato___Early_Blight.name: Early Blight
Tomato___Early_Blight.description: Early blight of tomato is caused by Alternaria solani. It is one of the most common tomato diseases, causing significant defoliation and fruit loss if left unmanaged.
symptoms: Dark brown to black lesions with concentric rings (target-board pattern) on older leaves
Yellow halo surrounding lesions
Lesions start on lower leaves and progress upward
Dark, sunken, leathery lesions may appear at the stem end of fruit
treatment: Apply fungicides (chlorothalonil, mancozeb, azoxystrobin) at first sign of disease
Remove and destroy infected lower leaves
Avoid overhead irrigation; water at the base of plants
Mulch around plants to prevent soil splash
Maintain good plant nutrition — stressed plants are more susceptible

Tomato___Healthy.name: Healthy Tomato
Tomato___Healthy.description: No disease detected. The tomato plant appears healthy with no visible symptoms of infection.
symptoms: No visible symptoms.
treatment: Continue standard agronomic practices
Monitor regularly for early signs of disease or pest pressure
Maintain proper staking, pruning, and irrigation

Tomato___Late_Blight.name: Late Blight
Tomato___Late_Blight.description: Tomato late blight is caused by Phytophthora infestans, the same pathogen responsible for potato late blight. It spreads with alarming speed in cool, wet weather and can destroy entire crops within days.
symptoms: Irregular, water-soaked, pale green lesions on leaves rapidly turning brown
White cottony sporulation on leaf undersides during humid conditions
Brown, greasy patches on stems that can girdle and kill the plant
Firm, brown, greasy rot on green or ripe fruits
treatment: Apply protectant fungicides (copper hydroxide, mancozeb) preventively
Use systemic fungicides (fluopicolide, cymoxanil) after early detection
Remove and destroy all infected plant tissue immediately
Avoid overhead irrigation and working in fields when plants are wet
Plant resistant varieties where available

Tomato___Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.name: Yellow Leaf Curl Virus
Tomato___Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.description: Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV) is a begomovirus transmitted by the silverleaf whitefly (Bemisia tabaci). It is one of the most damaging tomato viruses worldwide, causing massive yield losses in tropical and subtropical regions.
symptoms: Upward curling and yellowing of leaflet margins (cupping)
Severe stunting of new growth — young leaves are small and crinkled
Interveinal chlorosis giving leaves a mottled yellow-green appearance
Flowers drop before fruit set, causing near-total yield loss in severe cases
treatment: Control whitefly populations with insecticides (imidacloprid, thiamethoxam) or reflective mulches
Use TYLCV-resistant or tolerant tomato varieties as primary management
Remove and destroy infected plants immediately to reduce virus spread
Use fine mesh insect nets in seedling nurseries to prevent whitefly access
There is no cure once a plant is infected — prevention and vector control are critical
